content.text: Which positive integers can be written as the sum of two squares?

content.title: Fermat's Two-Square Theorem
content.content: An odd prime $p$ is a sum of two squares if and only if $p \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $p = x^2 + y^2$, then modulo 4, squares are 0 or 1, so 
$p \equiv 0, 1, \text{ or } 2 \pmod{4}$. Since $p$ is an odd prime, 
$p \not\equiv 0, 2$, so $p \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

content.text:
The sufficiency is harder; one approach uses the fact that $-1$ is a 
quadratic residue mod $p$ when $p \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

content.content: Expand both sides and verify equality.

content.text: This identity shows: if $m$ and $n$ are sums of two squares, so is $mn$.

content.title: Sums of Two Squares
content.content:
A positive integer $n$ is a sum of two squares if and only if in its 
prime factorization, every prime $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$ appears to an 
even power.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Classification
content.problem:
$50 = 2 \cdot 5^2 = 1^2 + 7^2$ $\checkmark$ (no primes $\equiv 3 \pmod 4$)

$21 = 3 \cdot 7$: both $3 \equiv 3$ and $7 \equiv 3$ appear to odd powers. 
Not a sum of two squares. $\times$

$45 = 3^2 \cdot 5$: $3^2$ has even exponent. $45 = 3^2 + 6^2$ $\checkmark$

content.title: Lagrange's Four Squares Theorem
content.content: Every positive integer is a sum of four squares.
content.label: Theorem
